text: This is a case of a fifty-three-year-old diabetic with acute cholecystitis. I was called in the morning, added her on for the afternoon. Again, I typically decompress my gallbladder almost every time when I can't get a good grasp on it. If I can get a good grasp, I don't like to do that as you can see because it starts to pour out fluid almost immediately.
The surgeon uses cautery to gently push down on the woody infundibulum of the gallbladder, following the edematous tissue plane.

The surgeon carefully applies small bursts of cautery to the edematous tissue near the lateral aspect, gradually pushing and sweeping it down to expose the infundibulum of the gallbladder, proceeding millimeter by millimeter.
The surgeon observes pericholecystic fluid and infundibular edema while gently navigating the surgical field, maintaining a high position on the gallbladder.

The surgeon visualizes the cystic duct clearly and identifies a posterior artery curling up but not entering the gallbladder. The surgeon continues to work on the posterior plane, ensuring safety in the dissection area.

The surgeon identifies the common bile duct and cystic ducts, noting the generous length of the cystic duct suitable for clip placement while maintaining a safe distance from the common bile duct. The infundibulum appears rigid and woody, making it difficult to manipulate or mold with surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues to search for the cystic artery, toggling the view back and forth without any visible signs of the artery in the current field.

The surgeon establishes the critical view of safety by administering additional ICG dye, which causes the cystic artery to fluoresce brightly, confirming its identification amidst surrounding structures.

The surgeon has established the triangle of Calot and the critical view of safety, preparing to clip the cystic duct and artery. The duct is cut first, ensuring secure placement of the clips on the structures.

The surgeon carefully removes the gallbladder from the edematous cystic plate, avoiding entry into the inflamed tissue due to acute cholecystitis. A one-centimeter rim of pericholecystic fluid is visible surrounding the gallbladder.
